coredata.dc:title: Engineering cross-linking by coal-based graphene quantum dots toward tough, flexible, and hydrophobic electrospun carbon nanofiber fabrics
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Jiayao
Zhang, Su
Wang, Luxiang
Jia, Dianzeng
Xu, Mengjiao
Zhao, Zongbin
Qiu, Jieshan
Jia, Lixia
coredata.dc:description: Polyacrylonitrile-derived electrospun carbon nanofiber fabrics (ECNFs) are believed to have great potentials in many aspects. However, the un-optimized/limited strength and flexibility considerably hinder their practical applications. We find that the tensile strength, Young's modulus, and flexibility of the ECNFs can be significantly improved by the simple addition of coal-based graphene quantum dots (CGQDs) to a spinning solution. The Young's modulus of the CGQD-added ECNF is enhanced by more than 7 times than that of pure polyacrylonitrile-derived one. However, the improvement is barely observed when oxygen-bearing functional groups on the CGQDs are removed by chemical reduction. Characterized by scanning electron microscopy; X-ray diffraction; and Raman, Fourier-transform infrared, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopies, we propose that the CGQDs act as cross-linking agents because of their abundant oxygen-bearing functional groups and good chemical reactivity, resulting in the formation of dense, strong, and flexible carbon skeleton. Moreover, the hydrophobicity of the ECNFs is also gradually improved with the increase in CGQD content. This is ascribed to the increase in diameter of the carbon nanofibers. The ECNF prepared under the optimized condition shows a water contact angle of approximately 142°. Thus, it can be used for efficient and endurable gravity-driven oil/water separation.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Program on Key Research Project ( 2016YFC0400504 ), the National Science Foundation of China ( 21671166 ), the Western Light Talent Culture Project of Chinese Academy of Sciences ( YBXM-2014-07 ), Ten Thousand Back-up Talent Program ( wr2015cx01 ), the Achievement Transformation Project of Xinjiang province ( 2016C03007 ), and the Fund of the State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering (No. CRE-2017-C-201 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the ECNF preparation procedure. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) and (b) HRTEM images of the CGQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) XRD, (b) Raman, (c) FT-IR, (d) XPS C1s , and (e) TG curves of the as-prepared CGQD and RCGQD. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
SEM images of (a1-3) ECNF-0, (b1-3) ECNF-0.3, (c1-3) ECNF-0.6, (d1-3) ECNF-0.8, (e1-3) ECNF-1.0, and (f1-3) ECNF-R.

(a) ECNF-1.0 paperboat; photographs of ECNF-1.0 (b) before and (c) after being twisted; (d) photograph of ECNF-0 after slight compression; and (e) the Young's modulus and (f) the stress-strain curves of the ECNFs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
XRD patterns of (a) the CGQD-added ECNFs and (b) the RCGQD-added ECNF and (c) Raman spectra of the as-prepared ECNFs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Cross-sectional SEM images of (a) ECNF-0, (b) ECNF-0.3, (c) ECNF-0.6, (d) ECNF-0.8, (e) ECNF-1.0, and (f) ECNF-R.
Water contact angles and nanofiber diameters of the ECNFs with different CGQD contents. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) FT-IR and (b) XPS C1s spectra of the as-prepared ECNFs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Water droplet (2 μL) on ECNF-1.0 with a WCA of 142.6 ± 1.1°; (b) toluene droplet (2 μL) on ECNF-1.0 with a contact angle of 0°; the WCA of ECNF-1.0 (c) after immersed in toluene for 5 days and (d) after heating at 150 °C in air for 12 h; (e) and (f) gravity-driven separation of oil water mixture using ECNF-1.0; (g) toluene penetration fluxes of the ECNFs; (h) fluxes of ECNF-1.0 toward several common organic solvents. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Relative concentrations of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen in the CGQD and the RCGQD estimated from the XPS survey spectra and the contribution of components in the area of XPS C1s spectra.

row.entry: Contribution of components in C1s spectra (%)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Relative concentrations of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen in the ECNFs estimated from the XPS survey spectra and the contribution of the components in area of XPS C1s spectra.

row.entry: Contribution of components in C1s spectra (%)

head.ce:title: Engineering cross-linking by coal-based graphene quantum dots toward tough, flexible, and hydrophobic electrospun carbon nanofiber fabrics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Energy Materials Chemistry, Ministry of Education, Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Materials, Autonomous Region, Institute of Applied Chemistry, Xinjiang University, Urumqi 830046, Xinjiang, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Energy Materials Chemistry
Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Lab of Fine Chemicals, School of Chemical Engineering, Liaoning Key Lab for Energy Materials and Chemical Engineering, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, Liaoning, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Lab of Fine Chemicals
Liaoning Key Lab for Energy Materials and Chemical Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Authors with equal contributions to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polyacrylonitrile-derived electrospun carbon nanofiber fabrics (ECNFs) are believed to have great potentials in many aspects. However, the un-optimized/limited strength and flexibility considerably hinder their practical applications. We find that the tensile strength, Young's modulus, and flexibility of the ECNFs can be significantly improved by the simple addition of coal-based graphene quantum dots (CGQDs) to a spinning solution. The Young's modulus of the CGQD-added ECNF is enhanced by more than 7 times than that of pure polyacrylonitrile-derived one. However, the improvement is barely observed when oxygen-bearing functional groups on the CGQDs are removed by chemical reduction. Characterized by scanning electron microscopy; X-ray diffraction; and Raman, Fourier-transform infrared, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopies, we propose that the CGQDs act as cross-linking agents because of their abundant oxygen-bearing functional groups and good chemical reactivity, resulting in the formation of dense, strong, and flexible carbon skeleton. Moreover, the hydrophobicity of the ECNFs is also gradually improved with the increase in CGQD content. This is ascribed to the increase in diameter of the carbon nanofibers. The ECNF prepared under the optimized condition shows a water contact angle of approximately 142°. Thus, it can be used for efficient and endurable gravity-driven oil/water separation.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrospun carbon nanofiber fabrics (ECNFs) with sufficient mechanical strength and flexibility show great potentials in many aspects such as smart membranes, flexible energy storage materials, and wearable electronic devices [1–5]. Polyacrylonitrile (PAN) is the most commonly used precursor to prepare ECNFs because of its high carbon yield, low cost, and easy processability [6–10]. However, the PAN-derived ECNFs always have un-optimized/limited mechanical properties, thus strongly inhibiting their practical applications [10–20]. To improve their mechanical properties, two methods were commonly used in previous works: increasing the carbonization temperature [10–12] and/or adding reinforced nanofillers [13–23]. High-temperature treatment (up to 3000 °C) can significantly enhance the graphitization degree and mechanical properties of the ECNFs. However, the graphitization procedure requires special equipment of high energy consumption [15,18]. Moreover, for the preparation of functional composites, most of the secondary phase cannot sustain such a high temperature. For adding nanofillers, thermally stable carbon nanomaterials with high aspect ratio such as carbon nanotubes and graphenes are considered as promising reinforcing candidates. Dzenis et al. [15] and other researchers [7,9,16] proposed that well-oriented carbon nanotubes in electrospun carbon nanofibers not only played as a reinforced phase to improve their mechanical properties but also facilitated the graphitization during thermal treatment. As a result, a highly crystalline graphitic structure is achieved with a small amount of globally dispersed carbon nanotubes at a relatively low temperature. Very recently, Li et al. added graphene nanoribbons into the electrospun carbon nanofibers and found similar results [17]. With abundant surface functional groups, graphene oxide and graphene oxide nanoribbons are also found to be effective reinforced nanofillers [18–21]. Researchers considered that the mechanical property improvement is attributed to (1) the strong interface interaction between the high aspect ratio carbon nanofillers and the PAN-derived carbon matrix and (2) enhanced graphitization degree of the carbon nanofibers by carbon nanofiller templating. However, the effects of functional groups were always ignored or underestimated [24].

Graphene quantum dots are a type of graphene derivations that have a lamellar size usually smaller than 10 nm. They have relatively low aspect ratio compared to carbon nanotubes and graphenes. They have attracted great attention in photoluminescence, bioimaging, biosensors, solar cells, and so on because of their unique physicochemical properties [25–29]. However, few works have been conducted on the electrospinning of graphene quantum dots into the carbon nanofibers. Tour et al. developed a novel chemical oxidation method for the large-scale preparation of graphene quantum dots from abundant coal resource, which greatly promoted their development [30–36]. In previous works, our group added chemical oxidized coal nanoparticles into the ECNFs for high-performance supercapacitor electrodes and oil absorption [37–40].

In this study, we find that the strength and flexibility of the ECNFs can be greatly improved by the simple addition of coal-based graphene quantum dots (CGQDs) to a spinning solution. From the detailed investigation, we propose that the mechanism is due to the high reactivity of the CGQDs. Abundant oxygen-bearing functional groups allow the CGQDs to act as cross-linking agents toward PAN molecules during carbonization, resulting in a dense, strong, and flexible carbon skeleton of the carbon nanofibers. Furthermore, the hydrophobicity of the ECNFs is also gradually improved along with the increase in CGQD content. This phenomenon is attributed to the increase in diameter of the carbon nanofibers. Good strength, flexibility, and hydrophobicity allow the ECNFs to be applied in many areas. As an exemplification, gravity-driven oil/water separation tests reveal that the ECNFs are promising candidates as effective and endurable separation membranes.

All the chemical agents were used as received without further purification. Bituminous coal powder (Heishan, Xinjiang, China) was used as the precursor. All the reactions were carried out in a fuming cupboard to avoid releasing the hazardous gases (e.g., NO2). The CGQDs were prepared by a chemical oxidation method according to Ref. [30] but with some modification. Aqueous hydrazine solution was used to reduce the CGQDs. The details are given in the Supporting Information.

The ECNF preparation schematic is given in Fig. 1 . To prepare the ECNFs, 0.4 g of the commercially available PAN powder (PAN, J & K Scientific Co. Ltd., average molecular weight: 150,000 Da) and a certain amount of the CGQDs (0, 0.12, 0.24, 0.32, and 0.4 g) or 0.4 g of the reduced CGQDs (RCGQDs) were fully dissolved into 5 mL of dimethyl formamide (AR) at 80 °C by vigorous stirring and sonication. The solution was then placed in a plastic syringe with a stainless steel needle (inner diameter: 1.12 mm). For electrospinning, a positive voltage of 18 kV and a negative voltage of −3 kV were applied to the spinneret and the roller collector, respectively. The distance between the spinneret and the collector was fixed at 18 cm. The roll speed of the collector was set at 80 rpm.

The obtained PAN-based fabrics were pre-oxidized in air at 280 °C for 1 h and then carbonized at 1000 °C for 1 h under N2 atmosphere at a heating rate of 2 °C min−1. The obtained ECNFs were named as “ECNF-ratio" in which the “ratio” indicates the ratio of CGQD to PAN. For example, the ECNFs prepared using 0.32 g CGQD and 0.4 g PAN was named as “ECNF-0.8”. A sample of the RCGQD added to the spinning solution was named as “ECNF-R".

The morphologies of the ECNFs were examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S-4800). The crystal structure of the products was measured by X-ray diffraction (XRD) recorded on a Rigaku D/max-2500B2+/PCX system operated at 40 kV and 20 mA using Cu Kα radiation. The Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was recorded using a Bruker VERTEX 70 Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer using attenuated total reflection attachment. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded on an ESCALAB 250 Xi using monochromatic Al Kα (1486.6 eV) X-ray source with 30 eV pass energy in 0.5 eV step over an area of 650 × 650 μm to the sample. The binding energy scale was internally calibrated to the 284.4 eV energy of the C1s peak. The C1s core level spectra were deconvoluted by fitting using the symmetric Gaussian/Lorentzian function after the subtraction of the background signal by Shirley's method. Raman spectra were recorded using a 532-nm laser on a Bruker SENTERRA dispersive Raman spectrometer. The thermogravimetric (TG) analysis was measured on a NETZSCH STA 449 F3 simultaneous thermal analyzer in an N2 atmosphere at a heating rate of 10 K min−1. The temperature range was from 25 to 800 °C. The contact angle was measured using an optical contact angle tester (OCAI5EC Dataphysics, German). The mechanical properties of the ECNFs were measured using a universal mechanical tester (H5KT).

The oil flux and the oil/water separation performance of the ECNFs were measured using a glass filter with a diameter of 5 cm. Several common organic solvents such as toluene, bromobenzene, ethyl acetate, trichloromethane, carbon tetrachloride, tetrahydrofuran, and petroleum ether were chosen as the exemplified oil phase. For clear observation during testing, a small amount of Oil Red O and methylene blue were added into the oil and water phases, respectively.

The CGQDs were prepared by the chemical oxidation of the bituminous coal using a mixture of HNO3 and H2SO4. The monodispersed “dark dots” with irregular shapes, which can be clearly identified in the HRTEM images given in Fig. 2 , are the as-prepared CGQDs. Some of them are marked by yellow circles in Fig. 2b. The diameter of the CGQDs ranged from approximately 2 to 4 nm.

Fig. 3 a shows the XRD patterns of the CGQD and the RCGQD. A broad and dispersive peak located at approximately 23° indicates the disordered stacking structure of the CGQDs. Generally, the main diffraction peak of graphene oxide located at approximately 10° is relatively narrow and sharp, indicating its layered stacking structure with an expanded interlayer space [41–44]. However, as the lamellar size decrease to quantum dot level, the low aspect ratio of the nanoparticles led to their disordered stacking rather than an anisotropic layered stacking (Figure S1). Removal of the surface functional groups led to a slight decrease in the CGQD interlayer spacing. Thus, the diffraction peak shifts to approximately 26° after reduction. The peaks located at approximately 1350 and 1580 cm−1 in the Raman spectra of carbons are identified as the D and G band, respectively. The ratio of the D peak to the G peak (ID/IG) reveals the defect density of carbon materials [45–48]. The ID/IG (calculated based on the peak area) of the RCGQD (1.69) is lower than that of the CGQD (2.01), indicating that parts of the defects are repaired during reduction (Fig. 3b). The peaks located at approximately 3500, 1720, 1580, 1520, and 1150 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 3c) indicate the presence of COOH, CO, CC, NO2, and COC in the samples [43,44,49,50]. The peaks of the oxygen-bearing functional groups are weakened in the spectrum of the RCGQDs, thus indicating the effective removal of the oxygen by chemical reduction.

The fitting results of the C1s spectra are given in Fig. 3d. The peaks located at approximately 284.6, 286.1, and 288.2 eV can be ascribed to the CC, CO/CN, and CO components, respectively [29,50]. The oxygen content and the CO component ratio decrease obviously. However, the nitrogen content and the CO/CN component ratio of the RCGQDs are higher than those of the CGQDs, as shown in Fig. 3d and Table 1 . This could be due to the doping of nitrogen in the RCGQDs during hydrazine reduction. This also reveals that the CGQDs have good chemical reactivity because of their small size and enriched edge structure [29,50,51]. The TG curves given in Fig. 3e further prove the effective reduction of the CGQDs.

The low, moderate, and high magnification SEM images of the ECNFs prepared with different CGQD contents are shown in Fig. 4 . The ECNFs are constructed by the random arrangement of the carbon nanofibers of uniform diameters (Fig. 4a1-f1). Their diameter distributions can be obtained statistically from Fig. 4a2-f2. The average diameters of the carbon nanofibers gradually increase from 141.5 to 773.3 nm with the increase in CGQD content, and the results are given in Fig. 8. It should be mentioned that the oxygen-enriched CGQDs considerably affected the diameters of the carbon nanofibers. On the contrary, the RCGQDs show a negligible effect on the diameters of the carbon nanofibers. The carbon nanofibers in ECNF-R (Fig. 4f1-f3) show a similar diameter distribution as those in ECNF-0.3 (Fig. 4b1-b3) even with a high RCGQD content. Moreover, from the high magnification SEM images given in Fig. 4a3-f3, almost all the carbon nanofibers exhibit a smooth surface. This result is important for us to discuss the mechanism of hydrophobicity improvement of the ECNFs, which will be presented later. The morphology of the electrospun nanofibers mainly depends on the viscosity of the spinning solution [52–55]. The viscosity of our spinning solution is gradually enhanced with the increase in CGQD content because of the strong interaction between the oxygen-bearing functional groups and the PAN molecules (Fig. 1) [14,15,23], thus increasing the diameter of carbon nanofibers. Removal of the oxygen-bearing functional groups weakens this interaction, thus causing the low viscosity of the RCGQD-added spinning solution, and barely changed the diameter of ECNF-R.

The CGQD-added ECNFs exhibit good strength and flexibility. They can be twisted or folded into other shapes (Fig. 5 a–c). After being unfolded, the fabric could restore to its original state without any cracks. However, the pure PAN-derived ECNF (ECNF-0) in our work is quite brittle that it can be easily broken into small pieces even by slight compression (Fig. 5d). The mechanical properties of the ECNFs are shown in Fig. 5e and f. The Young's modulus of the ECNFs slightly decrease from ECNF-0 to ECNF-0.3, and then, it gradually and significantly improve with the increase in CGQD content (Fig. 5e). The slight decrease is because the strain of ECNF-0.3 is more than 2 times higher than that of ECNF-0, but both has a similar tensile stress (Fig. 5f). ECNF-1.0 shows the highest tensile strength of approximately 2.2 MPa and Young's modulus of approximately 70 Mpa. These values are more than 7 times higher than those of the pure PAN-derived ECNF (ECNF-0). However, even with a large RCGQD content, the strength and Young's modulus of ECNF-R are lower than those of any as-prepared CGQD-added ECNFs (Fig. 5e). Therefore, we can conclude that (1) the addition of the CGQDs is an effective way to improve the flexibility, tensile stress, and Young's modulus of the ECNFs and (2) oxygen-bearing functional groups of the CGQDs play an important role for the improvement.

The XRD and the Raman patterns of the ECNFs are given in Fig. 6 . The broad and dispersive peak located at approximately 24° of the CGQD-added ECNFs (Fig. 6a) reveals their amorphous nature [56]. However, when the RCGQDs are added, ECNF-R shows a much narrow and sharp diffraction peak (Fig. 6b), indicating its higher crystallinity than that of ECNF-1.0. The Raman results (Fig. 6c) demonstrate that (1) the defect density of the ECNFs increases by the introduction of the CGQDs and (2) ECNF-R (0.99) shows a lower defective degree than ECNF-1.0 (1.01) [45–48]. In general, the mechanical properties of the electrospun carbon nanofibers can be improved by the formation of a less defective graphitic structure by high temperature treatment [10–12,15,17,18]. However, in our work, ECNF-1.0, which has a low crystallinity and the highest defect density, exhibits the best mechanical properties, while ECNF-R, which has the highest crystallinity, exhibits poor mechanical properties when compared with any CGQD-added ECNFs.

From the above discussion, the mechanical property improvement of the CGQD-added ECNFs cannot be attributed to the improved crystallinity or decreased defect density. In addition, the CGQDs have much smaller size and lower aspect ratio than graphenes and carbon nanotubes. Therefore, the common reinforcement mechanism based on the strong interface interaction between carbon matrix and nanofillers may not be suitable to explain our results [7,9,15–21]. For understanding the improvement mechanism, the cross-sectional SEM images of the carbon nanofibers are given in Fig. 7 . The carbon nanofibers with low CGQD content (ECNF-0 and ECNF-0.3, Fig. 7a and b) exhibit a loose and porous inner structure. An increase in the CGQD content results in the formation of a dense carbon skeleton (ECNF-0.6, ECNF-0.8, and ECNF-1.0; Fig. 7c, d, and e). Moreover, their mechanical properties are also gradually improved. The increase in diameter of the carbon nanofibers (Figs. 4, 7 and 8) may also be the reason for the mechanical property improvement. However, the carbon nanofibers of ECNF-0.3 and ECNF-R shows a similar diameter distribution (Fig. 4b1-3 and f1-3). The Young's modulus and the tensile stress of ECNF-0.3 are almost two times higher than those of ECNF-R (Fig. 5e and f). Therefore, the mechanical property improvement is mainly due to the formation of dense and strong carbon skeleton rather than the increase in fiber diameter. It should be noted that the carbon nanofibers in ECNF-R (Fig. 7f) remained as a loose and porous inner-structure even with a high RCGQD content. As mentioned above, we propose that the improvement mechanism is due to the cross-linking effect of the introduced CGQDs. Abundant oxygen-bearing functional groups of the CGQDs endow their high surface polarity and chemical reactivity. The strong interaction between oxygen-bearing functional groups and the polar PAN molecules induces the PAN molecules to be tightly adsorbed around the CGQD nanosheets [14,15,23], thereby leading to the formation of compact electrospun PAN fiber precursors. Moreover, the CGQDs also act as cross-linkers toward the PAN molecules during carbonization because of their good chemical reactivity originated from the oxygen-bearing functional groups, thus resulting in the formation of dense and non-porous carbon nanofibers with improved mechanical properties.

The hydrophobicity of the ECNFs is further investigated. We found that the water contact angle (WCA) of the ECNFs also gradually increased with the increase in CGQD content. This result is in accordance with the trend of the diameter change (Fig. 8 and Figure S2). The ECNF-1.0 shows the highest WCA of approximately 142°.

The good strength, flexibility, and hydrophobicity of the ECNFs ensure them to be used for gravity-driven oil/water separation [60]. For example, the optimized ECNF-1.0 has a WCA of 142.6 ± 1.1°, thus indicating its hydrophobic property (Fig. 10 a). In contrast, when toluene is dropped on its surface, it immediately spreads on the fabric with an oil contact angle of 0°, indicating the superoleophilicity of ECNF-1.0 (Fig. 10b). To test the stability of ECNF-1.0, it was immersed in toluene for 5 days or heated at 150 °C in air for 12 h. The WCAs of ECNF-1.0 barely changed even suffered from these treatments (Fig. 10 c and d), demonstrating its endurability and chemical stability. As a result, the toluene water mixture (100 mL/100 mL) was fully separated in a few minutes by a simple gravity-driven technique (Fig. 10 e and f). Toluene penetrated through the membrane, while water remained on its top surface. The separation efficiency was higher than 99.5%. The gravity-driven toluene fluxes of the different ECNFs are given in Fig. 10g. With the increase in CGQD content, the toluene flux significantly increased. ECNF-1.0 exhibits the highest flux of 324 L m−2 h−1. Moreover, the fluxes of ECNF-1.0 toward several common organic solvents such as bromobenzene, ethyl acetate, trichloromethane, carbon tetrachloride, tetrahydrofuran, and petroleum ether are given in Fig. 10h.

The strength, flexibility, and hydrophobicity of the ECNFs were significantly improved by the simple addition of the CGQDs to the spinning solution. ECNF-1.0 showed the highest tensile stress of approximately 2.2 MPa and Young's modulus of approximately 70 Mpa. This phenomenon was owed to the abundant functional groups and good chemical reactivity of the CGQDs, ensuring them to act as cross-linking agents for the formation of dense, strong, and flexible carbon skeleton. The WCAs of the ECNFs also gradually increased along with the increase in CGQD content. The improved hydrophobicity of the ECNFs was ascribed to the increase in diameter of the carbon nanofibers. ECNF-1.0 showed the best hydrophobicity and superoleophilicity. The ECNFs had good chemical stability, endurability, and high separation efficiency when used for gravity-driven oil/water separation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Program on Key Research Project (2016YFC0400504), the National Science Foundation of China (21671166), the Western Light Talent Culture Project of Chinese Academy of Sciences (YBXM-2014-07), Ten Thousand Back-up Talent Program (wr2015cx01), the Achievement Transformation Project of Xinjiang province (2016C03007), and the Fund of the State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering (No. CRE-2017-C-201).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2017.11.071.
